# CURRICULUM REASONING
# Generated by: Session 1 - May 25 2026
# Course: CMU 15-213
# Lab: L0 C Programming Lab
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

NEXT PROJECT: 2
  Why now          : Project 1 confirmed student thinks in variable names not addresses. The & operator was confused with pass-by-reference. Must solidify that addresses are real values before introducing pointers.
  Weakness targeted: Address vs value confusion appeared immediately in Project 1
  Teaching angle   : An address is a value you can store and pass around. Not magic. Not special syntax.
  Analogy          : House address written on a piece of paper. The paper holds the address. The house holds the contents.
  Watch for        : Student may still conflate & with pass-by-reference from other languages.

NEXT PROJECT: 3
  Why now          : After Project 2 student knows & gives an address. Now must see that an address can be stored in a variable. That variable is a pointer. Nothing magical.
  Weakness targeted: Pointers feel magical. Student will treat them as special. They are integers.
  Teaching angle   : A pointer is just an integer. Print it with %p and you see a number.
  Analogy          : A pointer is a piece of paper with a house address written on it.
  Watch for        : Student may think pointer type matters for what it IS rather than how it is used.

NEXT PROJECT: 4
  Why now          : Student knows a pointer holds an address. Now must see that * follows that address to the actual bytes. This is the operation everything else depends on.
  Weakness targeted: Confusing the pointer itself with what it points to.
  Teaching angle   : * means go to that address and read or write the bytes there.
  Analogy          : You have a paper with an address. * means physically go to that house and open the door.
  Watch for        : Student writing *p when they mean p or p when they mean *p.

NEXT PROJECT: 5
  Why now          : Student can dereference. Now must see the difference between moving the pointer and moving the value. This distinction breaks queue_insert_head if not owned.
  Weakness targeted: Conflating pointer reassignment with data mutation.
  Teaching angle   : Two operations that look similar but do completely different things.
  Analogy          : Changing which house your paper points to vs changing what is inside the house.
  Watch for        : Student reassigning head pointer and losing the old list.

NEXT PROJECT: 6
  Why now          : Student owns pointers. Now must see structs as byte blocks not abstract containers. queue_t and list_ele_t are structs. Cannot implement queue without this.
  Weakness targeted: Student thinks struct fields are separate variables floating in space.
  Teaching angle   : A struct is one contiguous block of bytes. Fields are named offsets into that block.
  Analogy          : A form with labeled boxes. All boxes are printed on one sheet of paper.
  Watch for        : Student surprised that sizeof struct includes all fields.

NEXT PROJECT: 7
  Why now          : Student knows structs. Now must own the arrow operator because every queue function uses it constantly. Arrow must feel mechanical not magical.
  Weakness targeted: Arrow operator feels like special syntax. It is not.
  Teaching angle   : p->field is (*p).field. Two operations you already know combined.
  Analogy          : Go to the address on the paper. Open the door. Look in the labeled box.
  Watch for        : Student using dot when they have a pointer. Using arrow when they have a value.

NEXT PROJECT: 8
  Why now          : Student owns structs and pointers. Queue stores char* strings. Must understand what a string physically is before allocating or copying one.
  Weakness targeted: Student treats strings as values not byte arrays.
  Teaching angle   : A string is an array of bytes ending in zero. Nothing more.
  Analogy          : A row of mailboxes. Each holds one character. Last one always holds zero.
  Watch for        : Student forgetting null terminator exists. Off-by-one on allocation.

NEXT PROJECT: 9
  Why now          : Student knows strings are byte arrays. Now must own strlen because malloc needs exact byte count. Off-by-one here causes buffer overflow that ASan catches.
  Weakness targeted: Off-by-one on string allocation. Most common bug in queue_insert_head.
  Teaching angle   : strlen returns length without the zero. malloc needs length plus one.
  Analogy          : strlen counts the letters in the word. malloc needs space for letters plus the period at the end.
  Watch for        : malloc(strlen(s)) instead of malloc(strlen(s) + 1). Will corrupt memory silently.

NEXT PROJECT: 10
  Why now          : Student knows how many bytes to request. Now must own malloc itself. Every queue node allocation uses malloc. NULL check is not optional.
  Weakness targeted: Student does not check NULL return from malloc.
  Teaching angle   : malloc can fail. When it fails it returns NULL. Dereferencing NULL crashes.
  Analogy          : Asking a bank for a loan. Bank might say no. You must check before spending.
  Watch for        : Missing NULL check after malloc in queue functions.

NEXT PROJECT: 11
  Why now          : Student can allocate. Must now own free. Every queue_free and queue_remove_head calls free. Misunderstanding free causes double-free and use-after-free.
  Weakness targeted: Student thinks free erases memory or makes it safe to read.
  Teaching angle   : free tells the system this memory is available again. The bytes are still there until overwritten.
  Analogy          : Checking out of a hotel room. Your stuff might still be there but the room belongs to someone else now.
  Watch for        : Student reading from pointer after free. Student calling free twice on same pointer.

NEXT PROJECT: 12
  Why now          : Student owns malloc and free separately. Must now see they must balance. One malloc one free. This is the rule ASan enforces. Leaks and double-frees both break this.
  Weakness targeted: Imbalanced malloc and free. Source of most memory bugs in the queue.
  Teaching angle   : Draw the balance. Every malloc gets a matching free. Count them.
  Analogy          : Every time you borrow something you must return it exactly once.
  Watch for        : queue_free missing free for string inside each node.

NEXT PROJECT: 13
  Why now          : Student knows malloc can return NULL. Must now own what NULL actually is and why dereferencing it always crashes. Every queue function checks for NULL queue.
  Weakness targeted: Student does not guard against NULL before dereferencing.
  Teaching angle   : NULL is address zero. The OS protects address zero. Touching it crashes.
  Analogy          : Address zero is a locked building. Going there gets you arrested immediately.
  Watch for        : Missing NULL check at start of every queue function.

NEXT PROJECT: 14
  Why now          : All tools are owned. Student can now implement string copying which is what queue_insert_head and queue_insert_tail actually do. This is the first real composition.
  Weakness targeted: Student stores pointer to caller string instead of making own copy.
  Teaching angle   : You do not own memory you did not allocate. Always copy strings you need to keep.
  Analogy          : Taking a photo of someone elses document instead of photocopying it. When they shred it you lose the information.
  Watch for        : Storing s directly instead of allocating and copying.

NEXT PROJECT: 15
  Why now          : Student is about to read queue.h which uses typedef extensively. Must understand typedef before reading the actual lab structs or confusion blocks progress.
  Weakness targeted: Student confused by list_ele_t vs struct list_ele.
  Teaching angle   : typedef is just a rename. No new behavior. list_ele_t is struct list_ele.
  Analogy          : A nickname. The person is the same. The name is shorter.
  Watch for        : Student thinking typedef creates a new type with different behavior.

NEXT PROJECT: 16
  Why now          : All foundations owned. Student now builds one linked list node by hand from scratch. This makes the abstract data structure physically real before implementing the queue.
  Weakness targeted: Student cannot picture what a node actually is in memory.
  Teaching angle   : A node is just a struct on the heap with a value and a next pointer.
  Analogy          : A box with a string inside and an arrow painted on the outside pointing to the next box.
  Watch for        : Student confused about why next is a pointer not a struct.

NEXT PROJECT: 17
  Why now          : Student has one node. Must now link two nodes together and see the chain physically. This is the exact operation queue_insert_head performs.
  Weakness targeted: Student does not see that linking is just assigning an address to a field.
  Teaching angle   : next is just a field. Setting it to another node address is just assignment.
  Analogy          : Drawing an arrow from one box to another. The arrow is the next pointer.
  Watch for        : Student allocating new memory when they should just be setting a pointer.

NEXT PROJECT: 18
  Why now          : Student can link nodes. Must now traverse a list with a loop. queue_free and queue_reverse both traverse. Recursive traversal stack-overflows at 1M nodes.
  Weakness targeted: Student writes recursive traversal. Forbidden at 1M elements.
  Teaching angle   : A cursor pointer walks the chain. While cursor is not NULL advance it.
  Analogy          : Walking a chain of connected rooms. Enter room. Note next door. Move through it. Repeat.
  Watch for        : Any recursive function that touches list nodes.

NEXT PROJECT: 19
  Why now          : Student is about to write malloc(sizeof(list_ele_t)). Must own sizeof first. sizeof pointer vs sizeof pointee is a reliable misconception.
  Weakness targeted: sizeof pointer vs sizeof pointee confusion.
  Teaching angle   : sizeof(int*) is 8. sizeof(int) is 4. The pointer and what it points to are different sizes.
  Analogy          : The address of a house fits on one line. The house itself is much larger.
  Watch for        : malloc(sizeof(list_ele_t*)) instead of malloc(sizeof(list_ele_t)).

NEXT PROJECT: 20
  Why now          : Student owns sizeof. Now must own the malloc(sizeof(T)) pattern which every queue function uses. This is the last foundation piece before Phase 2 implementation begins.
  Weakness targeted: Student hardcodes struct sizes or uses wrong sizeof argument.
  Teaching angle   : Always malloc(sizeof(the_struct_not_the_pointer)).
  Analogy          : Order a box big enough to fit the object not big enough to fit the label.
  Watch for        : Any hardcoded size in a malloc call.

NEXT PROJECT: 21
  Why now          : All 20 foundation projects complete. Student now implements the first real queue function. queue_new is the simplest — allocate and initialize. Sets the pattern for every function that follows.
  Weakness targeted: Student does not initialize fields after malloc. Uninitialized head pointer causes immediate crash.
  Teaching angle   : malloc gives you raw bytes. You must set every field yourself. Nothing is zeroed.
  Analogy          : You get an empty form. You must fill in every box. Blank boxes cause errors later.
  Watch for        : Missing head = NULL after malloc. Missing size = 0. Missing tail = NULL.

NEXT PROJECT: 22
  Why now          : Student can create a queue. Must now free one correctly. Order matters — free nodes before freeing the struct or you lose the pointer to the nodes forever.
  Weakness targeted: Student frees queue_t first then cannot reach the nodes.
  Teaching angle   : Free from the inside out. Nodes first. Struct last.
  Analogy          : Empty the house before demolishing it. Demolish first and everything inside is lost.
  Watch for        : Student freeing q before freeing q->head chain.

NEXT PROJECT: 23
  Why now          : Student can create and free. Now implements the first insert. queue_insert_head is the core operation. Every step must be in exact order or the list is corrupted.
  Weakness targeted: Student updates head before saving old head. Old list is lost.
  Teaching angle   : Save old head first. Point new node to old head. Then update head to new node.
  Analogy          : You are adding a new first car to a train. Attach it to the existing train first. Then declare it the new front.
  Watch for        : head = new_node before new_node->next = old_head. List lost permanently.

NEXT PROJECT: 24
  Why now          : Student can insert at head. Now must insert at tail in O(1). Naive implementation walks entire list. Fails at 1M elements. Tail pointer is the solution.
  Weakness targeted: Student walks list to find tail on every insert.
  Teaching angle   : tail pointer always points to last node. Update it on every insert. Never walk the list.
  Analogy          : A bouncer at the back of the line always knows who is last. No need to walk from the front.
  Watch for        : Any loop inside queue_insert_tail. Immediate O(n) failure.

NEXT PROJECT: 25
  Why now          : Student owns O(1) tail insert. Same pattern applies to size. Naive size counts nodes. Fails performance test. Stored counter is the solution.
  Weakness targeted: Student traverses list to count nodes on every size call.
  Teaching angle   : size field in queue_t is incremented on insert decremented on remove. Return it directly.
  Analogy          : You already derived this at session start — the supermarket manager who keeps a running count.
  Watch for        : Any loop inside queue_size. Immediate O(n) failure.

NEXT PROJECT: 26
  Why now          : Student can insert. Now must remove. queue_remove_head must save the string out before freeing the node. Order is critical. Free first means data is gone.
  Weakness targeted: Student frees node before copying string out of it.
  Teaching angle   : Copy the string out first. Then free the node. Never touch freed memory.
  Analogy          : Read the document before shredding it. Shred first and the information is gone.
  Watch for        : free(node) before strncpy(buf, node->value, bufsize).

NEXT PROJECT: 27
  Why now          : All six other functions work. Now implements reverse. Must flip pointers in place. No allocation allowed. Walk list once with three pointers.
  Weakness targeted: Student allocates new nodes violating lab constraint. Student loses list by not saving next before overwriting it.
  Teaching angle   : Three pointer technique. prev cur next. Save next before overwriting cur->next.
  Analogy          : Reversing a chain of arrows painted on boxes. Before repainting each arrow save where it currently points.
  Watch for        : Student allocating any memory inside queue_reverse. Immediate lab violation.

NEXT PROJECT: 28
  Why now          : All functions implemented. Now stress-tests every function with NULL queue and empty queue inputs. Both cases are distinct. Both must be handled in every function.
  Weakness targeted: Student handles one case but not the other. Traces crash on the unhandled case.
  Teaching angle   : NULL queue means q is NULL. Empty queue means q is valid but q->head is NULL. Different checks.
  Analogy          : NULL queue is a missing form. Empty queue is a blank form. Both exist. Both need handling.

NEXT PROJECT: 29
  Why now          : Student has a complete implementation. Now must learn to read ASan error reports. ASan is always running. Student must be able to find the bug from the report alone.
  Weakness targeted: Student cannot read stack trace and fixes wrong location.
  Teaching angle   : Frame 0 is innermost. Find first frame in your own code. That line is the bug location.
  Analogy          : A call stack is a list of return addresses. The crime scene is always in your code not in the library.
  Watch for        : Student fixing code in harness.c or qtest.c instead of queue.c.

NEXT PROJECT: 30
  Why now          : Everything is complete. Now runs all 15 traces with driver.py. This is the ground truth. Every trace must pass. Score is 100 or there is a remaining bug.
  Weakness targeted: Student passes some traces and cannot diagnose which function is failing.
  Teaching angle   : Each trace tests specific functions. Read the trace name. It tells you which function to debug.
  Analogy          : Each trace is a specific test. The name is the clue. trace-01-ops tests basic operations.
  Watch for        : Student guessing instead of reading the failing trace to identify which function is broken.
